planning_summary: The planned digital twin for CNC machining systems is focused on providing real-time data-driven insights and automated decision-making processes to optimize scheduling, maintenance, and quality control. The feasible capabilities identified support the R2 level of the 4R Framework, which involves replicating the physical system in a virtual environment.
gaps_to_reach_target: The digital twin purpose, scope, and supported decisions are explicitly defined. (no explicit evidence)
System boundaries, major components, inputs/outputs, and important states are identified. (partial evidence)
Critical variables are selected without major over-instrumentation or missing context. (no explicit evidence)
A repeatable data collection and storage pipeline exists. (partial evidence)
Collected data is structured, usable, and verified against known system behavior. (partial evidence)
action_items_to_reach_target: Define the digital twin's purpose, scope, and expected outcomes.: Write a detailed statement defining the DT purpose, target decisions, and expected outcomes. This will help in aligning all stakeholders on the goals of the project.

Document system boundaries, included/excluded components, and key interactions.: Create a detailed document outlining the system boundaries, including/excluding components, and key interactions. This will help in defining the scope of the digital twin.

Identify critical variables for data collection without over-instrumentation or missing context.: Identify the key properties, characteristics, and states that are essential for the digital twin. Ensure that the selected variables are neither overly complex nor insufficient to provide meaningful insights.

Design a repeatable data collection strategy.: Define the sensors, APIs, or software sources needed for data acquisition and specify their placement. Determine the communication protocols and synchronization requirements.

Develop an initial data representation layer.: Collect the data from the physical system, structure it, remove errors, and organize into a usable format. Verify and validate the data against known system outputs.

phases.level_description: In the Representation (R1) phase is, it is necessary to understand the physical system, decide on the significant features of the system for the use case, and create a system architecture for data collection and data storage from the physical component. In this first level of the framework, the foundational digital representation of the physical system is developed. An understanding of the inner working of the system is required to appropriately represent a system. This phase primarily considers the identification of key properties, characteristics and states that comprise the physical entity. Once the qualities and characteristics have been identified and categorized, the design of the processes to collect data that are both directly and indirectly associated with each property commences. This is the phase where decisions regarding the purpose of DT should be made since the purpose of the DT is critical to the development of the terminal DT phase and the implementation of the Representation is a critical component. A high-level DT implementation would likely not require high resolution data whereas a more complex DT will. Some characteristics of the representation phase include the physical system represented as data, the data flows from the physical device to the virtual device, and the data to be collected, stored, and verified. The data collected at this phase allows for understanding of the system and is used for analysis, visualization, and model creation. Considerations in this phase are: (i) Data collection pipeline (ii) Data persistence/storage, (iii) Data polling rate (iv) Data structures and format (v) Metadata (vi) information design modeling (vii) Data preprocessing pipelines (viii) Data analysis and representation. It is important to highlight that the representation process is dynamic and evolves with the implementation of the next phases of the framework. As requirements for the DT change over time, so does the data needed to support these capabilities. Representation should be designed in a modular and scalable way, so that new requirements are served in a plug and play manner, with minimal effect to the rest of the system.
phases.core_outcome: The physical system is clearly bounded, instrumented, and represented as reliable, structured data that is directly usable for analysis, visualization, and later model building.

workflow_steps.step_title: 1. Define the purpose, scope, and goal of the digital twin

tasks.task_text: 1.1 Explanation of why the digital twin is being created and what the expected outcomes are. Identify decision(s) the DT will support
tasks.example: The goal of the digital twin was to create a generic, reuseable, and customizable system for reseach and experimentation. The expected outcomes included monitoring, evaluation, analysis, and visualization of the 3D
tasks.implementation_status: partially covered in planning evidence, implementation still required

tasks.task_text: 1.2 Set the system boundaries: Define where the system starts and ends including machines, processes, and interactions are included in the model.
tasks.example: The system boundary was limited ot the componetns that directly impact the additive manufacturing process, inluding the printer, printing process, and environmental conditions. One example of a non-cirtical componets that was excluded was internal motor details
tasks.implementation_status: partially covered in planning evidence, implementation still required

workflow_steps.step_title: 2. Decompose and Understand the Physical System

tasks.task_text: 2.2 Identify the key properties, characteristics and states that comprise the physical entity. Break down the system into: Components/subsystems, Inputs/outputs, and States and behaviors. Identify critical variables (temperature, throughput, position, etc.) and interactions between components
tasks.example: The system was broken down into key components such as printing bed, orentation (x, y, z axis), G-code, and environmental conditions
tasks.implementation_status: partially covered in planning evidence, implementation still required

tasks.task_text:
2.3 Capture the static properties and dynamic states. Static varibles are the ones that remain constant and dynamic are variables that change over time. Static properties (geometry, configuration) and
Dynamic states (real-time behavior)
tasks.example: The dynamic components were identified as bed (Y-axis), the printer head (X-axis), the spools, pillars, and the printer head together (Z-axis). The static compoents were identified as frame of the 3D printer and the G-code
tasks.implementation_status: partially covered in planning evidence, implementation still required

workflow_steps.step_title: 3. Identify Key features and Representation Requirements

tasks.task_text:
3.1 Identify which properties actually matter for the use case
(avoid over-instrumentation).
tasks.example: The temperature (bed and extruder), postion (extruder and bed), and environmenta factors (humidity and temperature) were identified as the most important dynamic components
tasks.implementation_status: partially covered in planning evidence, implementation still required

tasks.task_text: 3.2. Define Required accuracy and update frequency
tasks.implementation_status: partially covered in planning evidence, implementation still required

tasks.task_text: 4.1 Determine the sensors/data sources that are needed and where they should be placed
tasks.example: The data was collected using thermocouple sensors for temperature, distance sensors for position, environmental sensors for humiditiy and temperature, and Octoprint to monitor the printer.
tasks.implementation_status: partially covered in planning evidence, implementation still required

tasks.task_text: 4.2 Define data aquisition methods (IoT devices, PLCs, APIs) and communication protocols (MQTT, OPC UA, etc.)
tasks.example: The data was collected using sensors attached to the Raspberry Pi and the data was transmited using APIs
tasks.implementation_status: partially covered in planning evidence, implementation still required

tasks.task_text: 4.3. Specify synchronization requirements and data storage. Choose storage solutions for Time-series databases (e.g., sensor data), Relational databases (structured data), and Data lakes (raw/unstructured data)
tasks.example: The data types collected from using external sesnros and Octoprint were JSON, CSV, and text files. The databases used were MongoDB and MySQL for storage
tasks.implementation_status: partially covered in planning evidence, implementation still required

tasks.task_text:
4.4 Define Data Structures, Formats, Data schemas (tables, time-series, JSON, etc.) and
Naming conventions
tasks.implementation_status: partially covered in planning evidence, implementation still required

tasks.task_text: 5.1. Collect the data from the physical system
tasks.example: The data was collected from sensors, cameras, and software applications during the printers operation
tasks.implementation_status: partially covered in planning evidence, implementation still required

tasks.task_text: 5.2. Structure the data, emove the erros, and organize into a usable format
tasks.example: The collcted dat was organized into cateogires of temperature, position, state, setting, and job data
tasks.implementation_status: partially covered in planning evidence, implementation still required

tasks.task_text: 5.3 Verify and Validate the data, by comparing collected data to known system outputs
tasks.example: Sensor data was compared to the data on the OctoPrint itself.
tasks.implementation_status: partially covered in planning evidence, implementation still required

tasks.task_text:
5.4 Key Representation check:
Can you explain how the system works using only the data you collect?
(If not, you’re missing critical variables or context.)
Is every data stream clearly tied to a system component, state, or decision?
(If something has no purpose, it shouldn’t be there.)
Is your data clean, structured, and immediately usable for analysis or modeling?
(If you have to “figure it out later,” R1 isn’t done.)
Does your data pipeline run reliably without manual intervention?
(If it’s fragile, it’s not a real representation.)
Does the level of detail match your intended use (not overbuilt, not underbuilt)?
(Too much or too little both mean R1 failed.)
tasks.implementation_status: partially covered in planning evidence, implementation still required

risks_or_notes: The current planning does not include detailed modeling or validation steps required for R3 (Reality) and R4 (Relational) levels, such as predictive models and autonomous decision-making. These will need to be addressed in future phases.

problem_statement: We need a digital twin of our CNC machining systems to provide accurate, data-driven insight into Quality, Cost, and Time before production begins. The digital twin must determine whether we have the capability and capacity to run a given job, optimize scheduling based on available equipment, and automate key decision-making processes that currently rely on manual judgment. It should continuously monitor degradation of cutting tools, spindles, and coolant, predict remaining useful life, and assess how component wear will impact part quality and throughput. By integrating real-time machine data with predictive simulation, the digital twin will enable proactive maintenance, reduce cycle time, extend asset life, and ensure that every job is run on the right machine, at the right time, with predictable and efficient performance.
tailored_planning_summary: The digital twin will provide real-time insights into Quality, Cost, and Time before production begins by determining the capability and capacity to run a given job, optimizing scheduling based on available equipment, and automating key decision-making processes. The current feasible capabilities support R1 (Representation) but need more detailed planning for R2.

tailored_action_items.section2_planning_summary: The digital twin will provide real-time insights into Quality, Cost, and Time before production begins by determining the capability and capacity to run a given job, optimizing scheduling based on available equipment, and automating key decision-making processes. The current feasible capabilities support R1 (Representation) but need more detailed planning for R2.

section4_tailored_4r_action_items.action_item: Define the digital twin's purpose, scope, and expected outcomes.
section4_tailored_4r_action_items.what_to_do: Write a detailed statement defining the DT purpose, target decisions, and expected outcomes. This will help in aligning all stakeholders on the goals of the project.
section4_tailored_4r_action_items.why_it_matters: A clear definition is crucial for guiding the development process and ensuring that the digital twin meets the business requirements.
section4_tailored_4r_action_items.use_case_specific_example: The digital twin aims to determine whether we have the capability and capacity to run a given job, optimize scheduling based on available equipment, and automate key decision-making processes currently relying on manual judgment.
section4_tailored_4r_action_items.expected_output_or_evidence: A one-paragraph statement defining the DT purpose, target decisions, and expected outcomes.
section4_tailored_4r_action_items.dependencies_or_gaps: Lack of clear business requirements and stakeholder alignment.

section4_tailored_4r_action_items.action_item: Document system boundaries, included/excluded components, and key interactions.
section4_tailored_4r_action_items.what_to_do: Create a detailed document outlining the system boundaries, including/excluding components, and key interactions. This will help in defining the scope of the digital twin.
section4_tailored_4r_action_items.why_it_matters: A well-defined system boundary is essential for ensuring that all relevant aspects are considered during development.
section4_tailored_4r_action_items.use_case_specific_example: The CNC machining systems include multiple machines across different locations, with specific components such as cutting tools, spindles, and coolant. The boundaries will exclude internal motor details but include environmental conditions affecting machine performance.
section4_tailored_4r_action_items.expected_output_or_evidence: A document detailing the system boundaries, included/excluded components, and key interactions.
section4_tailored_4r_action_items.dependencies_or_gaps: Lack of detailed component lists and system architecture diagrams.

section4_tailored_4r_action_items.action_item: Identify critical variables for data collection without over-instrumentation or missing context.
section4_tailored_4r_action_items.what_to_do: Identify the key properties, characteristics, and states that are essential for the digital twin. Ensure that the selected variables are neither overly complex nor insufficient to provide meaningful insights.
section4_tailored_4r_action_items.why_it_matters: Selecting appropriate critical variables is crucial for ensuring that the data collected is relevant and useful for analysis and modeling.
section4_tailored_4r_action_items.use_case_specific_example: For this CNC machining use case, apply this step to variables and records such as spindle speed, feed rate, spindle load, vibration, tool id, machine state.
section4_tailored_4r_action_items.expected_output_or_evidence: A list of critical variables identified for the digital twin.
section4_tailored_4r_action_items.dependencies_or_gaps: Lack of historical data and technical specifications.

section4_tailored_4r_action_items.action_item: Design a repeatable data collection strategy.
section4_tailored_4r_action_items.what_to_do: Define the sensors, APIs, or software sources needed for data acquisition and specify their placement. Determine the communication protocols and synchronization requirements.
section4_tailored_4r_action_items.why_it_matters: A structured data collection strategy ensures that the digital twin can reliably collect relevant data from various sources.
section4_tailored_4r_action_items.use_case_specific_example: For this CNC machining use case, apply this step to variables and records such as spindle speed, feed rate, spindle load, vibration, tool id, machine state.
section4_tailored_4r_action_items.expected_output_or_evidence: A detailed data collection strategy document.
section4_tailored_4r_action_items.dependencies_or_gaps: Lack of sensor specifications and communication protocol details.

section4_tailored_4r_action_items.action_item: Develop an initial data representation layer.
section4_tailored_4r_action_items.what_to_do: Collect the data from the physical system, structure it, remove errors, and organize into a usable format. Verify and validate the data against known system outputs.
section4_tailored_4r_action_items.why_it_matters: A well-structured and validated data representation is essential for ensuring that the digital twin can provide accurate insights.
section4_tailored_4r_action_items.use_case_specific_example: For this CNC machining use case, apply this step to variables and records such as spindle speed, feed rate, spindle load, vibration, tool id, machine state.
section4_tailored_4r_action_items.expected_output_or_evidence: A structured and validated dataset ready for analysis and modeling.
section4_tailored_4r_action_items.dependencies_or_gaps: Lack of collected sensor data and known system outputs.
